meta.title: Architecture Design Game
meta.description: A browser-based security architecture game that teaches players to choose secure design decisions.

scenarios.title: Online Shopping Architecture
scenarios.summary: A local retail store wants to launch an online shopping application for delivery and product browsing.
requirements: Prevent unauthorized users from easily logging into customer accounts.

choices.label: Multi-Factor Authentication Login
choices.feedback: Adds a strong extra layer against unauthorized access, even if it reduces convenience.

choices.label: Voice-recognized Login
choices.feedback: Improves security over a password alone, but can be bypassed with a convincing voice recording.

choices.label: Single Password-based Login
choices.feedback: A single password does not provide strong protection against unauthorized logins.

choices.label: HTTPS
choices.feedback: Protects data in transit and is the correct baseline for web application traffic.

choices.label: Simple HTTP
choices.feedback: HTTP sends data without transport encryption, exposing sensitive information in transit.

choices.label: Triple DES
choices.feedback: Stronger than plaintext but outdated compared with modern web transport encryption standards.

choices.label: Hashed Password plus Bcrypt
choices.feedback: Password hashing with bcrypt protects stored credentials far better than saving raw values.

choices.label: Binary Encryption
choices.feedback: Encryption is better than plaintext, but this choice is vague and weaker than strong password hashing practices.

choices.label: Plain English Text
choices.feedback: Plaintext storage leaves sensitive data directly readable to attackers.

choices.label: Role-Based Access Control
choices.feedback: RBAC fits this application by separating customer and employee capabilities cleanly.

choices.label: Attribute-Based Access Control
choices.feedback: Flexible, but easier to misconfigure for a simpler retail application than RBAC.

choices.label: Rule-Based Access Control
choices.feedback: Static rules alone are a poor fit here and can become restrictive without improving security enough.

choices.label: Centralized Logging plus Alerts
choices.feedback: Centralized monitoring and alerts improve detection, response, and troubleshooting.

choices.label: Structured Logging
choices.feedback: Useful for machine-readable analysis, but less complete than centralized monitoring with alerts.

choices.label: No Logging
choices.feedback: Without logs, security incidents and operational failures are harder to detect and investigate.

scenarios.title: Hospital Information Database
scenarios.summary: A local hospital manages sensitive patient records and must comply with state privacy laws.
requirements: Protect confidential patient data from attackers.
Ensure data availability in life-critical situations.
Maintain audit trails for system activity.

choices.label: Smart Cards plus Biometrics
choices.feedback: Combines possession and biometric factors for strong authentication in a sensitive environment.

choices.label: Password-Based
choices.feedback: A password alone is too weak for protecting highly sensitive medical records.

choices.label: Certificate-Based
choices.feedback: Stronger than passwords alone, but does not match the layered assurance of smart cards plus biometrics.

choices.label: End-to-End Encryption
choices.feedback: Provides the strongest protection so only intended communicating parties can read sensitive data.

choices.label: Partial Encryption
choices.feedback: Better than none, but it still leaves portions of patient data exposed.

choices.label: Virtual Private Network
choices.feedback: A VPN protects traffic paths but is not a substitute for direct patient data encryption.

choices.label: Attribute-Based Access Control
choices.feedback: ABAC supports contextual decisions for staff, device, and environment in a hospital setting.

choices.label: Policy-Based Access Control
choices.feedback: Useful, but less adaptive than full attribute-based decisions for sensitive medical access.

choices.label: Mandatory Access Control
choices.feedback: Highly rigid controls are a poor fit here and can obstruct urgent care workflows without improving outcomes enough.

choices.label: Encrypted Backup
choices.feedback: Protects backup copies while preserving availability for recovery during critical events.

choices.label: Incremental Backup
choices.feedback: Helpful for recovery, but the scenario description treats it as weaker protection than fully encrypted backups.

choices.label: No backups needed
choices.feedback: No backups leave life-critical data at risk of permanent loss after attack or failure.

choices.label: Full Audit Logging
choices.feedback: Complete audit trails support accountability, incident review, and regulatory compliance.

choices.label: Centralized Logging
choices.feedback: Centralized logs help operations, but full audit logging is the stronger fit for healthcare compliance.

choices.label: Partial Logging
choices.feedback: Incomplete records make investigation and accountability unreliable during a breach or misuse event.
